statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
Monocarp has an integer $ n $ .

He wants to represent his number as a sum of three distinct positive integers $ x $ , $ y $ , and $ z $ . Additionally, Monocarp wants none of the numbers $ x $ , $ y $ , and $ z $ to be divisible by $ 3 $ .

Your task is to help Monocarp to find any valid triplet of distinct positive integers $ x $ , $ y $ , and $ z $ , or report that such a triplet does not exist.

Input Format
The first line contains a single integer $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 10^4 $ ) — the number of testcases.

The only line of each testcase contains a single integer $ n $ ( $ 1 \le n \le 10^{9} $ ).

Output Format
For each testcase, if there is no valid triplet $ x $ , $ y $ , and $ z $ , print NO on the first line.

Otherwise, print YES on the first line. On the second line, print any valid triplet of distinct positive integers $ x $ , $ y $ , and $ z $ such that $ x + y + z = n $ , and none of the printed numbers are divisible by $ 3 $ . If there are multiple valid triplets, you can print any of them.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
4
10
4
15
9
Output
YES
4 5 1
NO
YES
2 8 5
NO

Note
In the first testcase, one of the valid triplets is $ x = 4 $ , $ y = 5 $ , $ z = 1 $ . None of these numbers are divisible by three, and $ 4 + 5 + 1 = 10 $ .

In the second testcase, there is no valid triplet.

In the third testcase, one of the valid triplets is $ x = 2 $ , $ y = 8 $ , $ z = 5 $ . None of these numbers are divisible by three, and $ 2 + 8 + 5 = 15 $ .

In the fourth testcase, there is no valid triplet.

solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
1886A - Sum of Three
You can solve this problem with some case analysis, but I'll describe another solution.
Suppose we want to iterate on every $$$x$$$, and then on every $$$y$$$, and then calculate $$$z$$$ and check that everything's OK. Obviously, this is too slow.
But we can speed this up: we don't have to iterate on all possible values of $$$x$$$ and $$$y$$$. Suppose $$$x  \lt  y  \lt  z$$$; then, if $$$x \ge 4$$$, we can decrease $$$x$$$ by $$$3$$$ and increase $$$z$$$ by $$$3$$$, until $$$x$$$ becomes either $$$1$$$ or $$$2$$$. The same goes for $$$y$$$: if $$$y \ge 7$$$, we can decrease it by $$$3$$$ and increase $$$z$$$ by $$$3$$$, until $$$y  \lt  7$$$. So, we only have to iterate on $$$x$$$ up to $$$2$$$, and on $$$y$$$ up to $$$5$$$. This yields a solution in $$$O(1)$$$.
You don't have to iterate on $$$x$$$ up to
exactly
$$$2$$$ and on $$$y$$$ up to
exactly
$$$5$$$, just use an upper bound for which you don't check too many possible triples of $$$x$$$, $$$y$$$ and $$$z$$$.

solutions.title: Solution (fcspartakm)
solutions.content:
#include
<
bits
/
stdc
++.
h
>
using
namespace
std
;
int
n
;
inline
void
read
()
{
cin
>>
n
;
}
inline
void
solve
()
{
for
(
int
x
=
1
;
x
<=
20
;
x
++)
{
for
(
int
y
=
1
;
y
<=
20
;
y
++)
{
if
(
x
+
y
>=
n
||
x
==
y
)
continue
;
int
z
=
n
-
x
-
y
;
if
(
z
==
x
||
z
==
y
)
continue
;
if
(
x
%
3
==
0
||
y
%
3
==
0
||
z
%
3
==
0
)
{
continue
;
}
puts
(
"YES"
);
cout
<<
x
<<
' '
<<
y
<<
' '
<<
z
<<
endl
;
return
;
}
}
puts
(
"NO"
);
}
int
main
()
{
int
t
;
cin
>>
t
;
while
(
t
--){
read
();
solve
();
}
}
